title: Demand and Supply
summary:
## Where this fits

Almost everything in microeconomics is an application of demand and supply. If you can *confidently* shift these two curves and read off what happens to price and quantity, then elasticities, taxes, subsidies, price controls, market failure and even the macro AD/AS model all become variations on the same skill. So it is worth going slowly here.

## What a market actually is

A **market** is any arrangement that brings **buyers** and **sellers** together — it doesn't need a physical place (think of eBay, or the global oil market). In a **free market**, nobody is *in charge* of setting the price. Instead the price emerges from the tug-of-war between what buyers want and what sellers offer. That self-organising process is called the **price mechanism**, and understanding it is the whole point of this chapter.

### The three jobs prices do (the price mechanism)
When a good becomes scarcer or more wanted, its price rises, and that single number quietly does three jobs at once:
1. **Rationing** — a higher price discourages some buyers, so the limited supply goes to those willing and able to pay. The good is *rationed* without anyone queuing or issuing coupons.
2. **Signalling** — the price is a message. A high price *signals* to producers ‘this is wanted here — bring more’; a low price signals ‘stop, resources are wasted here’.
3. **Incentive** — a higher price means higher potential profit, which *rewards* producers for responding. 
Adam Smith called this the **‘invisible hand’**: no central planner is needed, yet resources still flow toward what society wants.

## Demand — the buyers' side

**Demand** is the quantity of a good that consumers are **willing *and* able to buy** at each price over a period of time. Both words matter: wanting a Ferrari isn't demand unless you can pay. This is why economists say **effective demand**, not mere desire.

### The law of demand
> As the price of a good rises, the quantity demanded falls, *ceteris paribus* (all else equal).

The demand curve therefore slopes **downward**. But *why* does it slope down? Don't just memorise it — there are three real reasons, and examiners reward the explanation:

- **The substitution effect.** When a good gets more expensive, rival goods now look cheaper *by comparison*, so some buyers switch away from it. If the price of Coca-Cola doubles, more people buy Pepsi. This relative-price change reduces the quantity demanded.
- **The income effect.** A price rise makes your money go less far — your *real income* (purchasing power) falls — so you can afford less of the good even if your wage hasn't changed. A fall in price does the reverse: you feel slightly richer and buy more.
- **Diminishing marginal utility.** Each extra unit you consume gives you a bit *less* satisfaction than the one before (the first slice of pizza is bliss; the fifth, not so much). Since you value later units less, you'll only buy them if the price drops. So the willingness to pay — the height of the demand curve — falls as quantity rises.

### The single most important distinction: movement vs shift
This is where most marks are won or lost, so be ruthless about it.
- A change in **the good's own price** → a **movement *along*** the demand curve. If quantity demanded rises we call it an **extension**; if it falls, a **contraction**. Nothing else moved; you simply slid to a new point on the *same* curve.
- A change in **any other factor** → the **whole curve shifts** left or right. At *every* price, buyers now want a different amount.

> Golden rule to never get this wrong: **the good's own price can NEVER shift its own demand curve.** Price is on the axis — a change in it is a movement along, always.

### The conditions of demand (what shifts the curve) — mnemonic PIRATES
Don't just list these; understand the direction each pushes the curve.
- **P**opulation / demographics — more people (or an age group that wants the good) → demand shifts **right**.
- **I**ncome — for a **normal good**, higher income shifts demand **right**; for an **inferior good** (e.g. supermarket value ranges, bus travel), higher income shifts it **left** because people trade up.
- **R**elated goods — a rise in the price of a **substitute** shifts *this* good's demand **right** (buyers switch in); a rise in the price of a **complement** (e.g. petrol for cars) shifts it **left**.
- **A**dvertising & tastes — successful advertising or a fashion swing shifts demand **right**.
- **T**axes & subsidies on consumers — a subsidy to buyers shifts demand right; a tax on buyers shifts it left.
- **E**xpectations — if buyers expect prices to rise soon, they buy now → demand shifts **right** today.
- **S**easons / weather — sun cream demand shifts right in summer.

## Supply — the sellers' side

**Supply** is the quantity producers are **willing and able to sell** at each price over a period.

### The law of supply
> As price rises, quantity supplied rises, *ceteris paribus*.

The supply curve slopes **upward** for two linked reasons: (1) a higher price means higher potential **profit**, an incentive to produce more; and (2) as firms expand output, the **marginal cost** of each extra unit tends to rise (they work resources harder), so they need a higher price to make that extra unit worthwhile.

### Conditions of supply (what shifts the curve) — mnemonic PINTSWC
- **P**roductivity — higher output per worker lowers unit costs → supply shifts **right**.
- **I**ndirect taxes — a tax raises costs → supply shifts **left** (up).
- **N**umber of firms — more firms in the market → supply **right**.
- **T**echnology — better tech lowers costs → supply **right**.
- **S**ubsidies — a subsidy to producers → supply **right** (down).
- **W**eather / shocks — a drought hits an agricultural crop → supply **left**.
- **C**osts of production — a rise in wages, raw materials or energy → supply **left**.

Same discipline as demand: a change in the good's **own price** is a movement along supply; anything else shifts the whole curve.

## Putting them together: equilibrium

**Equilibrium** is the price where the plans of buyers and sellers match exactly — the quantity demanded equals the quantity supplied, and the market **clears**. There is no tendency to change, which is why it's called equilibrium (‘equal balance’).

### *How* the market gets there (the bit textbooks rush)
Markets reach equilibrium through **disequilibrium pressure**:
- If the price is **too high** (above equilibrium), sellers offer more than buyers want → a **surplus (excess supply)**. Unsold stock piles up, so sellers **cut prices** to shift it. Price falls until the surplus disappears.
- If the price is **too low** (below equilibrium), buyers want more than sellers offer → a **shortage (excess demand)**. Buyers compete and bid the price up; sellers, seeing eager buyers, **raise prices**. Price rises until the shortage disappears.
The price keeps adjusting until, and only until, demand = supply. That self-correction *is* the price mechanism in action.

### A worked numerical example
Suppose the market for a good has these schedules:

| Price ($) | Quantity demanded | Quantity supplied |
|---|---|---|
| 6 | 90 | 40 |
| 7 | 80 | 60 |
| **8** | **70** | **70** |
| 9 | 60 | 80 |
| 10 | 50 | 100 |

- At **$6**: Qd (90) > Qs (40) → a **shortage of 50** → upward pressure on price.
- At **$10**: Qs (100) > Qd (50) → a **surplus of 50** → downward pressure on price.
- At **$8**: Qd = Qs = 70. This is the **equilibrium**: price $8, quantity 70. The market settles here.

### A worked shift example (how to answer an exam scenario)
*‘A successful advertising campaign hits the market for electric cars, while at the same time battery costs fall. Analyse the effect on price and quantity.’*
1. **Advertising** improves tastes → **demand shifts right** (D1→D2). On its own: price ↑, quantity ↑.
2. **Lower battery (input) costs** → **supply shifts right** (S1→S2). On its own: price ↓, quantity ↑.
3. **Combine them:** both effects raise **quantity**, so quantity *definitely* rises. But demand pushes price up while supply pushes it down → the effect on **price is indeterminate** — it depends on which shift is larger. 
Stating that clearly (‘quantity rises; the change in price is ambiguous because it depends on the relative size of the two shifts’) is exactly what earns the top evaluation marks.

## Common mistakes to avoid
- Writing ‘demand goes up’ when the good's *own* price changes. That's an **extension of demand (a movement)**, not a rise in demand.
- Drawing a shift when you mean a movement (or vice versa) — the examiner reads your *diagram* as your answer.
- Forgetting that when **both** curves move, you can usually sign only *one* of price/quantity, not both.
- Confusing substitutes and complements, or normal and inferior goods, when applying PIRATES.

## Real-world application
- **Supply shock:** the 2022 surge in global **wheat and energy prices** (Russia–Ukraine war) shifted the supply of food and fuel **left** worldwide → higher prices, lower quantity.
- **Demand shift:** young, growing populations in **India and Nigeria** keep demand for housing and food shifting **right**; ageing **Japan** sees some demands fall.
- **Both at once:** in the **oil market**, OPEC+ supply cuts (S left) alongside reviving **Chinese** demand (D right) push price up while leaving the change in quantity ambiguous — a perfect ‘both curves shift’ case.

## Why it matters

Every later topic reuses this exact machinery: elasticities measure *how much* the curves respond; taxes and subsidies *shift supply*; price controls *stop the market clearing*; market failure is a *wrong* equilibrium. Get shifting curves clean and labelled now, and the rest of the course becomes far easier.

keyTerms.definition: Any arrangement bringing buyers and sellers together to trade; it need not be a physical place.
How prices ration, signal and incentivise to allocate resources in a free market (Adam Smith's ‘invisible hand’).
Demand backed by the willingness AND ability to pay, not mere desire.
As a good's price rises, its quantity demanded falls, ceteris paribus.
As a good's price rises, buyers switch to now-relatively-cheaper alternatives, lowering quantity demanded.
A price change alters real purchasing power, changing how much of the good buyers can afford.
A movement along the demand curve caused by a change in the good's own price.
A normal good's demand rises with income; an inferior good's demand falls as income rises.
Substitutes are consumed instead of each other; complements are consumed together.
As price rises, quantity supplied rises, ceteris paribus, due to higher profit incentive and rising marginal cost.
The market-clearing price where quantity demanded equals quantity supplied.
When price is below equilibrium so quantity demanded exceeds quantity supplied, pushing price up.
When price is above equilibrium so quantity supplied exceeds quantity demanded, pushing price down.

diagrams: **Basic equilibrium:** Price on the vertical axis, Quantity on the horizontal; downward D, upward S. Mark equilibrium price P* and quantity Q* at the intersection. Always label axes, both curves and the equilibrium point.

**Shortage & surplus:** draw a horizontal price line above equilibrium (label the horizontal gap ‘excess supply / surplus’) and one below (label ‘excess demand / shortage’), then show arrows for the direction price moves to correct each.
**Demand shift:** show D1 → D2 (right or left) along a fixed supply curve; mark the new equilibrium and use arrows to show how P and Q change.
**Supply shift:** show S1 → S2 along a fixed demand curve; a producer tax shifts S up/left, a subsidy or lower costs shift it down/right.
**Both curves shift:** show D and S both moving right; illustrate that Q clearly rises but the change in P is ambiguous — a key evaluation diagram.
tips: **Label every diagram fully** — axes (Price, Quantity), all curves (D, S, D1, D2…) and the equilibrium price & quantity. Unlabelled diagrams throw away easy marks; the examiner treats your diagram as part of the answer.
Use exact language: *‘the demand curve shifts right from D1 to D2’*, not ‘demand goes up’. **Shift vs movement** is the single most tested distinction — never confuse them.
For ‘analyse/explain’ questions, **chain the logic**: cause → which curve shifts and why → new equilibrium → effect on price AND quantity. Marks follow the chain, not the conclusion alone.
Beware the **own-price trap**: a change in a good's own price is always a movement along its own curve, never a shift.
When **both curves shift**, state that the effect on price *or* quantity is **indeterminate** and explain it depends on the relative size of the shifts — a reliable evaluation point.
Learn to **read a schedule**: at any price, compare Qd and Qs — if Qd > Qs there's a shortage (price rises); if Qs > Qd there's a surplus (price falls); where they're equal is equilibrium.
Bring in **varied real examples** (global wheat/oil shocks, population growth in India/Nigeria, housing) for application and evaluation marks.
